title: Lesson 366
transcript: Hi, we've reached domain number eight in this very long CISSP course. And as you can see here, domain number eight is focused on software development security. And one of the things that we have to highlight is that even this is called software development security, it really doesn't do justice to what the exam outline contains within domain number eight. Because if you look at these words here, it implies that obviously security needs to be focused on the development of applications and software, and that's certainly true. But once applications are put into production, the job of security is not finished. And that's what these words imply here. A better name for this domain is actually applications security. And this is something that I have made ISE Squared aware of, and they had promised years ago to change it, but for some reason they haven't just yet. But regardless of what it's called. And you'll probably hear me referencing application security throughout because it's a better way to describe the contents of not only this domain but also the exam outline and therefore the questions. And that implies, in fact, we're going to talk about in section or module number one when we get to it, that security needs to be involved not only in the development process, but the entire system lifecycle, which includes operations and decommissioning of the application at some point in the future. All right, so as usual we're going to start off with showing you the modules that are directly aligned with the exam outline. All right, so we have five modules here. that the exam outline contains. And again, there's always value in looking at the exact wording because that's where the questions come from. So let's start with 8.1, which is what we just talked about. Security needs to be involved in the development process right at the start and throughout each of the phases. And regardless of the methodologies that your organization may be using for software development, you need to involve security at the start. And in fact, some of the latest We've been talking about things like agile development and we've got CI and CD pipelines and we've got SEC DevOps and so on. A lot of these newer technologies concepts really force security to be involved in the process because as you know the proper way to address security requirements is to design them into something based on requirements as you start something. And that's certainly true of applications and software and anything that is driven through systems by code, for example. All right, so that's what this first section deals with. We need to bake security in. We need to design security into the application right at the offset based on the requirements, which implies that obviously we need to understand the requirements to begin with. and that's why security again has to be at the table right from the start understanding the requirements and how it impacts the ability of for example the organization to protect its assets its reputation its ability to be able to meet compliance requirements and other things that obviously equate to very high value. All right. So just as a summary, we've said this. a million times as well. So what you're seeing here on this slide is a typical software development lifecycle. And these phases that you see here in purple is what represents the phases that any application goes through in order to be developed. And as we just said, we need to involve security right at the start, at the initiation phase. And if you look at what that includes, that's where you involve management, for example, so you can start addressing their needs. but you're also looking at the methods that you're going to use. But security needs to involve here because we need to understand how this application that we are going to, for example, design and build and put into production, may affect the ability of the organization to protect its assets and meet compliance requirements and protect, for example, the privacy of our clients and customers. So it's pretty important to understand those right off the bat and only security can bring that to the table. And then as we start, for example, designing requirements and designing the specifications for code and modules, and then we have our developers develop them and they'll test them, and then we put finally something into production. there are specific items that security needs to do right from the start and throughout each of these phases and so it's important to be involved right at the start that's nothing new now what the industry refers to this of course is what's called the sdlc the systems development life cycle It's the phases that we go through in order to develop applications and software that needs to include security. The entire system lifecycle includes two other phases that you see in a different color here. So once applications have been developed, we need to put them into production. That's what we typically refer to as the operations phase. But even during that phase, security has to be involved because we're going to support things like logging and monitoring. We're going to be doing testing and assessments. We're going to be involved in supporting patch management and configuration management and so on. There's all kinds of things that security does during the operations phase and even the last phase of the system lifecycle. which as you can see here is what they refer to as the decommissioning or disposal phase. At some point in the future, when that application has run its course, and we need to either throw it out or replace it with something newer. There may be things that security needs to do, like for example, secure data destruction, secure media destruction, or maybe if there's a new system that's replacing the old one, we need to port information over in a secure way based on its value. All right, so on the exam, important to obviously focus on keywords. If they are talking about the software development lifecycle, it includes the phases up to and including. you know, putting something into production. If they're talking about the system lifecycle, that includes the entire lifecycle phases of the application itself, including operations and decommissioning. All right. And again, what we said is security needs to be involved right from the start and throughout. Now what if you're an organization that does not develop your own code? Maybe you buy code or software or applications or you contract out to some development company to do it for you. Should security be involved? And the answer to that of course is yes, right from the start and throughout. So what just showed up on your slides there, you know, initiation, development and acquisition, implementation and so on, are the phases that you would go through if you're buying software. instead of developing it on your own. And if you have some development company develop it for you, you would still have to involve security right at the initiation phase. So regardless of whether you develop your own code or you buy it, security has to be involved right from the start. Now, if you remember back to an earlier domain, we introduced concepts called certification and accreditation. And you'll remember where we talked about that, but it was in the reference of evaluation criteria. Evaluation criteria, just to remind you, are measurement systems that we can use to have confidence in vendor products for example that we are interested in buying because that evaluation criteria can give us a measurement, an independent and objective measurement of the security capabilities of specific type of products and services that we may be interested in buying from different vendors. Right, so that's what the concept of evaluation criteria systems are. But we talked about the concepts of certification and accreditation and we define those for you. Certification is defined as the technical, the comprehensive technical analysis of a solution, for example, or a product or a service to make sure that it meets your needs based on your requirements. And that's where security would be involved in the certification aspects of things. Because if we're talking about certification related to applications, we need to provide management some sort of confidence and approval and verification and validation that that software, that application that we are about to put into production does have the right level of security based on requirements so that again we can protect our assets we can allow the organization to meet its compliance requirements we can meet the requirements of privacy laws and legislation and payment cards and all kinds of other things that that application may be impacting so before we put something into production security has to provide certification right because that's the comprehensive technical analysis of something to make sure from our case that it has the right level of security and then of course if you remember back You know, accreditation is defined as the official management sign off and decision to operate something. And that can only be done by management. So the questions on the exam may relate to where would certification and accreditation be done in relation to software and applications that we may be buying or developing ourselves. And of course, that would be done as part of the implementation phase or the deployment phase or the putting into production phases. That's what we need to provide assurance that that application has the right level of security to protect the organization itself. All right, so that's where that goes. Now, what you're seeing on this slide is what's been referred to as the waterfall methodology for software development. And the reason it's called waterfall is because it's a phased approach to software development. You can't go on to the next phase until you've completed the previous one. And of course before you can go on to the next phase, there are approvals and sign-offs that need to get done by owners and management and so on. But the reason it's called waterfall is because just like water falling in a waterfall, we need to go through phases in order to get to the bottom. So that's the waterfall methodology, the phased approach. But here's the problem with it and why we've really come up with versions of this that address some of the fundamental problems of waterfall, including this big problem. And that is, as you start going into the phases of software development, Invariably, the owner who is paying for this is going to come up with new ideas. They're going to say things like, oh, wait a minute, that's not the functionality that I wanted. It's actually this. Or I just learned about something. Can we add this functionality into the system? and or can we add this or can we change this but you know waterfall doesn't allow you to go back water does not go backwards in a waterfall And so what we typically say to the owners is, you know, no, we can't. We can't go back and redesign things. But don't worry, owner, we'll be able to change things. Once we put this program into production or this application into production, you'll be able to change anything that you want through change control. or change management, which obviously costs money and time as well. So it's not ideal. And this is why the industry has really come up with new methodologies for software development over the years. And what you see here are just some examples. You know, anytime you see a list just like this one, it's not meant to be a complete or all-encompassing list, but just some of the methodologies that we've evolved into that really try and address some of those problems. that we had with waterfall so that we can go back and redesign things for example that's what the spiral method allows us to go back we can spiral back to phases and be able to add functionality and new requirements for example even though we may be well into the project without really affecting you know timelines and costs and so on and you know clean room for example that that talks about how there's nothing in the environment you know that can impact the quality and integrity of what's being produced you know from a coding perspective and so on and and structured programming where we have the idea of modules that are called from others that have some basic functionalities and so on. And of course you've all heard of Agile because that is something that is really exciting in the industry and of course we've been using for a number of years now. But the bottom line out of all of these is that it doesn't matter what methodologies you're using. Security has to be involved. They have to be knowledgeable in that methodology and how security fits in, but they need to be involved right from the start and throughout. In fact, if you look at Agile and you compare it to Waterfall, which is what we're trying to do here with this slide, you're going to see that there are many similarities, right? I mean, agile is very appropriately called because it allows us to be very agile where we structure things in sprint teams where we're totally focused on what we need to do every day, for example, because we have team members that are, for example, very skilled, highly motivated and they're shielded from anything that can impact. you know, what's going on so that we can sprint to the finish line and be able to achieve results very quickly and on time and on budget and with very high quality. as well all right but when you look at the two they're basically the same as well security needs to be involved as part of that so that we can include the security requirements all right and in fact you know there is a specific item in the exam outline that talks about the responsibilities of a very important component of Agile, and that is the Scrum Master. And the best way to think of a Scrum Master is a coach, for example, right? But when you look at some of these responsibilities that a scrum master has, they're really nothing new, right? I mean, even though this concept of agile is, you know, somewhat new in the industry, when you look at the principles that are pushed through this, they're not revolutionary. They're things that we've always been interested in. Like for example, it talks about how the Scrum Master shields team from external influences. removes barriers and facilitates close cooperation, improves productivity, and so on. Well, if we talk to management and say things, oh, we've got this new way of doing things, it's called agile, and it pushes things like shielding team members from external influences and removes barriers and promotes close cooperation and communication, management is going to look at you and say, well, what were we doing before? Why are these such novel ideas that are just revolutionary now? And I guess the answer to that is the technologies, the tool sets, the environments that we can use today for especially building code and producing code can do this very well and can push in a streamlined way. a lot of these principles so that we can create code that is very high quality but also includes security built in.
